Blue tiger:
Scientific Name: Tirumala limniace (Cramer, 1775)

Family: Nymphalidae (Danainae — Crows & Tigers)
Origin: South Asia & Southeast Asia
Most Commonly Found: India, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Southeast Asia
Wingspan: 90–100 mm

Distinguishing Feature: Dark brown to black wings patterned with translucent bluish-white spots and streaks. The distinctive blue hue is produced by the pigment pterobilin. Males congregate to sequester pyrrolizidine alkaloids from plants, converting them into pheromones used in courtship.
Habits & Habitat: Found in open vegetation, scrub jungles, and forest edges across tropical and subtropical zones. Exhibits gregarious migratory behaviour in southern India, sometimes forming massive roosts with other danaid species. Feeds on nectar from Lantana, Crotalaria, and Heliotropium. Larvae feed exclusively on plants of the family Asclepiadaceae.

Did you know? The Blue Tiger stores toxins sequestered from its larval host plants, rendering it deeply unpalatable to predators — a survival strategy shared across the entire Danainae subfamily.
Carpenter bee:
Scientific Name: Xylocopa spp. (Latreille, 1802)

Family: Apidae (Subfamily: Xylocopinae)
Origin: Worldwide; ~500 species across 31 subgenera
Most Commonly Found: Tropical & subtropical regions globally; ~15 species in India
Size: Up to 25 mm; one of the largest native bees

Distinguishing Feature: Robust, predominantly black body with a characteristically shiny, hairless abdomen — the key visual distinction from bumblebees. The genus name Xylocopa translates to "wood-cutter," a direct reference to their nest-building behaviour. Males of some species are covered in golden-green fur and bear a distinctive white or yellow face.
Habits & Habitat: Nest in dead wood, bamboo, or structural timber by excavating perfectly circular galleries using powerful mandibles. Found across forests, gardens, and wooded areas. Important pollinators; employ "buzz pollination" (sonication) to vibrate pollen loose from flowers. Adults are generalist foragers, active in the early morning. Some species occasionally "rob" nectar by piercing the base of corolla tubes.

Did you know? Carpenter bees are among the longest-lived bees, surviving up to three years. Daughters often overwinter in the family nest alongside their mother, forming primitive social colonies.
Cicada:
Scientific Name: Family Cicadidae (Latreille, 1802)

Family: Cicadidae; over 3,000 described species worldwide
Origin: Every continent except Antarctica
Most Commonly Found: Tropical & temperate forests worldwide; abundant in India
Lifespan: 2–17 years (most underground as nymphs)

Distinguishing Feature: Large, stout-bodied insects with prominent compound eyes, short antennae, and two pairs of clear, membranous wings. Males possess specialised drum-like organs called tymbals on the abdomen that produce their iconic, far-carrying calls — choruses can reach 80–100 decibels, rivalling a jackhammer.
Habits & Habitat: Nymphs live underground for years, feeding on xylem sap from tree roots at depths of 15–60 cm. Upon maturity, they emerge, moult into winged adults, mate, and die within weeks. Adults feed by piercing woody stems with straw-like mouthparts. Found in any habitat with deciduous trees — forests, parks, and urban gardens alike.

Did you know? Cicada wings are waterproof and antimicrobial at the nanoscale. The microscopic structure of their wing surfaces physically ruptures bacterial cell membranes on contact — a natural antibiotic architecture.
Common gull:
Scientific Name: Cepora nerissa (Fabricius, 1775)

Family: Pieridae (Yellows & Whites)
Origin: Sri Lanka, India, through Southeast Asia to Indonesia
Most Commonly Found: Lowland dry deciduous forests, thorn scrub, and coastal hinterlands of India
Wingspan: ~55–70 mm

Distinguishing Feature: A small to medium pierid butterfly. Males are white above with a bluish-grey suffusion at the wing bases along the veins, while females display seasonal polymorphism — wet-season forms differ markedly from dry-season forms in the extent of dark scaling. The larval foodplant is Capparis (caper family).
Habits & Habitat: A lowland species associated with thorn scrub, dry savannah woodlands, and open areas within deciduous forests. Males engage in "mud-puddling," congregating on wet sand or dung to absorb mineral salts essential for reproduction. When not feeding, adults perch with wings closed at the tops of bushes. Multivoltine, breeding throughout the year with peak activity from July to December.

Did you know? The Common Gull completes its entire life cycle in as few as 21 days, one of the faster turnarounds among Indian pierids, enabling it to build large populations rapidly during favourable seasons.
Common mormon:
Scientific Name: Papilio polytes (Linnaeus, 1758)

Family: Papilionidae (Swallowtails)
Origin: South & Southeast Asia, east to Japan and Indonesia
Most Commonly Found: Gardens, orchards, lightly wooded country across tropical Asia; very common in India
Wingspan: 90–100 mm

Distinguishing Feature — Mimicry: The classic textbook example of Batesian mimicry in India. While males are uniform velvety black with white hindwing spots, females occur in multiple polymorphic forms — each mimicking a different toxic, red-bodied swallowtail: form stichius mimics the Common Rose; form romulus mimics the Crimson Rose. Females even replicate the slow, deliberate wingbeats of their toxic models. A single gene — doublesex — governs the entire mimicry complex.
Habits & Habitat: Prefers lightly wooded country but thrives in gardens, citrus orchards, and urban greenery. Most abundant during the monsoon and post-monsoon months. Males are restless, fast-flying visitors to flowers; females are more deliberate in flight when in mimetic form. Larval host plants are citrus and related species in the family Rutaceae.

Did you know? The Common Mormon's name alludes to polygamy — its "many wives" are the several radically different female forms that coexist within the same population, each safeguarded by a different toxic model.
Common tiger:
Scientific Name: Danaus genutia (Cramer, 1779)

Family: Nymphalidae (Danainae — Crows & Tigers)
Origin: India, Sri Lanka, Myanmar extending to Southeast Asia & Australia
Most Commonly Found: Scrub jungle, deciduous forest edges, and fallow land across India; also called the Striped Tiger
Wingspan: 70–95 mm

Distinguishing Feature: Rich tawny-orange wings with distinctive broad black veins and two rows of white spots along the black wing borders. Closely resembles the Monarch butterfly of the Americas, reflecting shared Danainae ancestry. Males bear a prominent androconial pouch on the hindwing. In arid regions, the orange fades toward white, approaching the appearance of the White Tiger (D. melanippus).
Habits & Habitat: Thrives in scrub jungles, deciduous forest edges, and degraded hillsides in areas of moderate to heavy rainfall. Flight is slow and undulating with shallow wingbeats. Adults congregate at Crotalaria and Heliotropium plants to consume pyrrolizidine alkaloids, which are converted into pheromones and toxins. Famously fakes death when captured by predators; its tough, leathery body protects it from bites.

Did you know? The Common Tiger is itself mimicked by the Indian Tamil Lacewing, the Leopard Lacewing, and female Common Palmflies — making it one of the most influential Müllerian models in the Indian subcontinent.
Common wanderer:
Scientific Name: Pareronia hippia (Fabricius, 1787)

Family: Pieridae (Yellows & Whites)
Origin: Indian subcontinent; found in India & Southeast Asia
Most Commonly Found: Forest edges and open country across peninsular India
Wingspan: ~60–80 mm

Distinguishing Feature: Males are distinctive: pale blue with all veins defined in black and broad black apical borders on the forewings. Females are polymorphic. The common female form closely mimics the Glassy Tiger (Parantica aglea), a chemically protected danaid, to deter predation — a remarkable case of Batesian mimicry in a Pieridae species. The rare philomela form, reported chiefly from northeast India, is yellow-tinged at the wing base.
Habits & Habitat: Frequents forest edges, wooded clearings, and open country throughout its range. The species is notable for the striking sexual dimorphism between male and female — the two sexes appear almost unrelated at first glance. Larvae feed on Capparis species within the caper family, a foodplant shared with the Common Gull.

Did you know? The Common Wanderer is one of the few Pieridae butterflies in which the female, rather than the male, has evolved elaborate mimicry of a toxic species — an evolutionary investment driven by the females' slower flight and greater vulnerability to predation.
Crimson rose:
Scientific Name: Pachliopta hector (Linnaeus, 1758)

Family: Papilionidae (Red-bodied Swallowtails — Genus Pachliopta)
Origin: Endemic to southern India & Sri Lanka; range extends along eastern India
Most Commonly Found: Common along the Western Ghats; scrublands, forest patches, parks, and urban areas up to 2,400 m
Wingspan: 90–120 mm

Distinguishing Feature: A large, dramatically coloured swallowtail. The body is vivid crimson-red — an aposematic signal advertising toxicity. Bluish-black wings carry an interrupted white band on the forewings and a row of crimson crescent spots on the hindwings. Protected by law in India. The female form of the Common Mormon (Papilio polytes romulus) is its primary Batesian mimic.
Habits & Habitat: At home in both dense jungle and open country. Close to the ground, its flight is slow and fluttering; at height, it flies faster and stronger. Extremely fond of Lantana flowers, which are essential for nectar and egg production. Displays strong migratory tendencies — thousands can congregate during peak season before dispersing. Breeds up to seven times per year, completing the egg-to-adult cycle in just 39–47 days. Larvae feed on Aristolochia species, sequestering aristolochic acids that make the adult toxic.

Did you know? The Crimson Rose is legally protected under the Wildlife Protection Act of India — one of the few butterflies to carry such legislative status, reflecting its ecological and scientific significance as a primary mimicry model.
Death_shead hawk moth:
Scientific Name: Acherontia lachesis / A. atropos (Fabricius, 1798 / Linnaeus, 1758)

Family: Sphingidae (Hawk Moths)
Origin: A. lachesis across South & Southeast Asia (including India); A. atropos across Africa, Europe & West Asia
Most Commonly Found: Agricultural land, gardens, and forest edges throughout tropical Asia; near beehives
Wingspan: 80–130 mm

Distinguishing Feature: The most iconic moth in the world, instantly identifiable by the skull-shaped yellow marking on the thorax that gives it its name. Brown-patterned forewings provide camouflage against bark; hindwings are striking orange-yellow with dark bands. Unlike nearly all other Lepidoptera, this moth can produce an audible squeak by forcing air through its pharynx — a sound that mimics the piping of a queen honeybee.
Habits & Habitat: A boldly nocturnal species. Adults are notorious for raiding beehives to steal honey, protected by a thick, waxy cuticle and a chemical pheromone mimicking the scent of honeybees. Larvae are large, colourful hornworms feeding on nightshade family plants including potato, Jasminum, and Datura. A powerful migrant — individuals have been recorded far out to sea.

Did you know? Made famous by its appearance on the movie poster of The Silence of the Lambs (1991), the Death's Head Hawk Moth has been a symbol of the macabre in folklore, literature, and cinema for centuries — yet it is entirely harmless to humans.
Dragonfly:
Scientific Name: Infraorder Anisoptera; ~3,000 species worldwide

Family: Order Odonata; 11 families in Anisoptera
Origin: Ancient — fossil record extends 325 million years; found on every continent except Antarctica
Most Commonly Found: Near freshwater — ponds, rivers, marshes, and paddy fields worldwide; many species widespread in India
Wingspan: 2.5 cm – 19 cm (species dependent)

Distinguishing Feature: Enormous compound eyes with nearly 24,000 facets providing almost 360° vision. Two pairs of independently articulated wings enabling hovering, backward flight, and rapid directional changes at speeds exceeding 50 km/h. Many species display brilliant iridescent or metallic structural colouration. Dragonflies hold their wings flat at rest — the key distinction from the closely related damselflies (Zygoptera).
Habits & Habitat: Adults are supreme aerial predators with a hunting success rate of up to 95% — the highest of any animal studied. Nymphs are fully aquatic, breathing through gills in the rectum, and can remain in water for up to several years. Both adults and nymphs are voracious predators of mosquitoes, midges, and other insects. Wetland loss threatens populations globally — 16% of species are currently at risk of extinction.

Did you know? The Globe Skimmer (Pantala flavescens) makes an annual multigenerational migration of approximately 18,000 km — one of the longest known migrations of any insect — crossing the Indian Ocean between India and East Africa.
Emigrant:
Scientific Name: Catopsilia pomona (Fabricius, 1775)

Family: Pieridae (Yellows & Whites)
Origin: South & Southeast Asia, through the Philippines to Australia
Most Commonly Found: Ubiquitous across open areas, secondary forest, grasslands, river courses, and even deserts in India
Wingspan: 40–65 mm

Distinguishing Feature: Named for its striking migratory habits. Occurs in multiple seasonal colour forms: pale chalky white (pomona form) and bright lemon yellow with a silvery underwing cell (crocale form). These morphs are not separate species but are determined by photoperiod and temperature during larval development. The form catilla displays dark patches on the underside. All forms share a characteristic rapid, bouncy, irregular flight, often high above the tree canopy.
Habits & Habitat: Abundant throughout the year wherever its larval host plants — species of Cassia and Senna — are found. A powerful, fast-flying migratory species capable of crossing open sea. Larvae are bright leaf green; the pupa is suspended by a silk girdle and ends in a distinctive snout-like projection. Active and common in both wild habitats and urban gardens.

Did you know? The Common Emigrant's multiple colour forms (morphs) were once classified as entirely separate species by early entomologists — a classification that stood for over a century before genetic research confirmed them as a single, highly plastic species.
Eurema(Grass yellows):
Scientific Name: Eurema hecabe (Linnaeus, 1758)

Family: Pieridae (Yellows & Whites)
Origin: Pantropical — Africa, South Asia, Southeast Asia, Australia & the Pacific
Most Commonly Found: Open grasslands, scrub, roadsides, forest clearings, and gardens throughout India; one of the most abundant Indian butterflies
Wingspan: 30–40 mm

Distinguishing Feature: Bright sulphur-yellow wings with distinctive black forewing tips and a black border. Exhibits strong seasonal variation — the wet-season form has broad black borders while the dry-season form shows greatly reduced markings, giving the two forms a very different appearance. Flight is characteristically low, close to the ground.
Habits & Habitat: Found in virtually any open, sunny habitat — grasslands, scrub, forest clearings, roadsides, and agricultural borders. Frequents the ground level. Larvae feed on Cassia, Bauhinia, and other legumes. Consistently one of the most abundant butterfly species in surveys across India. Adults mud-puddle and engage in communal roosting.

Did you know? The Common Grass Yellow is so widespread and successful that it serves as a standard reference species in ecological research — its abundance acts as a reliable indicator of overall butterfly diversity and habitat health across a landscape.
Eudocima(fruit piercing moth):
Scientific Name: Eudocima phalonia / Eudocima spp. (Billberg, 1820)

Family: Erebidae (Subfamily: Erebinae, Tribe: Ophiderini)
Origin: Tropical Asia and the Pacific; E. phalonia widely distributed from India to Australia
Most Commonly Found: Fruit orchards and forest edges of tropical India, Sri Lanka, and throughout South & Southeast Asia
Wingspan: ~80–110 mm

Distinguishing Feature: Camouflaged, bark-like forewings contrast dramatically with vivid orange-and-black hindwings. Adults are equipped with an unusually robust, hardened, saw-like proboscis tip capable of piercing the tough skin of ripe fruit — a trait unique among Lepidoptera and highly destructive to commercial orchards. The wound left in the fruit allows secondary fungal and bacterial infections to develop rapidly.
Habits & Habitat: Strictly nocturnal. Adults feed on the juices of over 100 fruit species — including mandarin, orange, mango, banana, papaya, and lychee — damaging both cultivated and wild plants. Larvae feed harmlessly on Menispermaceae and Fabaceae host plants in forest habitats. Adults rest concealed on tree bark during the day, relying on their cryptic wing pattern for protection.

Did you know? Unlike most fruit-feeding moths that rely on fallen or rotting fruit, Eudocima uses its reinforced proboscis to attack healthy, intact fruit still on the tree — making it one of the few moths capable of directly causing pre-harvest fruit loss in orchards.
Genus eupterote:
Scientific Name: Eupterote spp. (Hübner, 1820)

Family: Eupterotidae (Monkey Moths); ~14 species recorded in India
Origin: South Asia, Southeast Asia, and the Oriental region
Most Commonly Found: Tropical and subtropical forests of India, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, and parts of Southeast Asia
Wingspan: 60–90 mm (species dependent)

Distinguishing Feature: Medium to large moths in warm brownish-yellow to ochre tones with intricate wavy black cross-lines and postmedial bands across the wings. Bodies are densely clothed in long, silky hair, lending them the popular name "monkey moths." Adults do not feed — they live entirely on fat reserves accumulated during the larval stage, a trait characteristic of the Eupterotidae.
Habits & Habitat: Nocturnal and strongly attracted to light. Found in tropical and subtropical forests, plantation edges, and degraded woodlands. Larvae of several species — including E. orientalis — are occasionally recorded as sporadic defoliating pests on economically important crops such as banana, cardamom, coffee, and Eugenia in South India. The family has a complex and still-evolving taxonomy, with significant species remaining undescribed.

Did you know? Eupterotidae adults have completely non-functional mouthparts — they cannot feed at all as adults, making reproduction the sole purpose of their brief above-ground existence. All energy reserves for flying, mating, and egg-laying are carried over from the larval stage.
Paper wasp:
Scientific Name: Polistes spp. (Latreille, 1802)

Family: Vespidae (Subfamily: Polistinae) — the single largest genus in Vespidae with 200+ species
Origin: Cosmopolitan; greatest diversity in the tropics; numerous species throughout India
Most Commonly Found: Gardens, forest edges, under building eaves, and on human structures across South Asia
Body Length: 1.5–2.5 cm

Distinguishing Feature: Slender-bodied, elongated wasps, typically yellow and black or brown and yellow in colouration. Named for the distinctive paper-like material of their nests, constructed by chewing plant fibres mixed with saliva into a pulp. The umbrella-shaped, open-celled nest is built without any outer casing, making the hexagonal brood cells directly visible. Unlike honeybees, Paper Wasps do not store honey.
Habits & Habitat: Eusocial insects forming colonies of typically 1–20 individuals (smaller than those of hornets or honey bees). Queens and workers share reproductive duties — a semi-primitive form of sociality. Dominant foragers feed larvae chewed caterpillars, making them important natural pest controllers in gardens and forests. Nests are typically built in sheltered locations on human structures, under leaves, or on tree branches. Can sting repeatedly when the nest is threatened.

Did you know? Paper Wasps can recognise the individual faces of nestmates — a sophisticated cognitive ability previously thought to require a large brain. They are among the few non-vertebrate animals demonstrated to have face recognition, using it to establish and maintain colony dominance hierarchies.
Plain tiger:
Scientific Name: Danaus chrysippus (Linnaeus, 1758)

Family: Nymphalidae (Danainae)
Origin: Africa, South Asia, Southeast Asia, and Australia — one of the world's most widespread butterflies
Most Commonly Found: Open areas — deserts, grasslands, urban gardens, and scrubland throughout India; avoids dense jungle
Wingspan: 70–80 mm

Distinguishing Feature: Bright brownish-orange wings with black borders dotted with white spots, and black forewing tips with a white band. Males possess a conspicuous androconial hair-pencil patch on the hindwing, used in courtship pheromone release. Unpalatable to most predators by sequestering cardenolide toxins and pyrrolizidine alkaloids from larval host plants (milkweed family). Its bright colouration serves as an aposematic warning.
Habits & Habitat: The strongest flier in the Danaus genus. Found across an extraordinary range of habitats from desert to tropical forest, up to 3,000 m elevation. Prefers arid, open conditions. Slow, gliding, undulating flight. Adults are mimicked by multiple species including the Danaid Eggfly (Hypolimnas misippus females) and the Leopard Lacewing — one of India's most-mimicked butterfly species.

Did you know? The Plain Tiger holds a unique place in art history: a 3,500-year-old Egyptian fresco at Luxor depicts what is believed to be this species — making it the oldest known butterfly illustration in human history.
Pyrochorrid bug:
Scientific Name: Dysdercus spp. / Pyrrhocoridae (Dahl, 1900)

Family: Pyrrhocoridae (Fire Bugs / Red Bugs); 300+ species worldwide
Origin: Pantropical; Dysdercus cingulatus common across tropical Asia including India
Most Commonly Found: Cotton fields, Hibiscus plantations, and wild Malvales vegetation across India and tropical Asia
Body Length: 12–18 mm

Distinguishing Feature: Boldly coloured insects, typically red or orange with black-and-white wing markings — the vivid colouration is aposematic, advertising chemical defences. Lack the simple eyes (ocelli) found on the top of the head, a key character distinguishing them from similar-looking families. Antennae consist of four segments, the second longer than the third. Many species in the genus Dysdercus are known as "cotton stainers" because their piercing mouthparts introduce staining fungi into cotton bolls.
Habits & Habitat: Primarily granivorous and fructivorous, feeding on seeds and fruits of plants in the order Malvales, particularly Malvaceae (cotton, okra, Hibiscus). Often found in large, gregarious colonies. A few species are secondary predators. Communicate through chemical pheromones. The family's greatest diversity is in tropical regions, with members found from the tropics extending into temperate Europe.

Did you know? When disturbed, Pyrrhocorid bugs release complex volatile chemical secretions from thoracic glands. These compounds serve both as alarm pheromones that alert nearby colony members and as chemical deterrents against predators.
Rhinoceros bettle:
Scientific Name: Subfamily Dynastinae (MacLeay, 1819)

Family: Scarabaeidae (Subfamily Dynastinae); 1,500+ species across 225 genera
Origin: Worldwide in tropical and subtropical regions; Oryctes rhinoceros (Coconut Rhinoceros Beetle) widespread across tropical Asia including India
Most Commonly Found: Tropical forests, decaying logs, and coconut/oil palm plantations across South & Southeast Asia
Size: Up to 15 cm (largest species); O. rhinoceros typically 4–5 cm

Distinguishing Feature: Named for the impressive rhinoceros-like horns borne exclusively by males — comprising one horn on the head and a second projecting forward from the thorax. Horn size is a direct indicator of nutritional health during development. The body is covered by a thick, rounded exoskeleton; some species have a striking metallic sheen. Despite their formidable appearance, rhinoceros beetles are entirely harmless — they cannot bite or sting.
Habits & Habitat: Adults are largely nocturnal and strongly attracted to light. Males use their horns in combat for access to feeding sites and mates. Larvae develop in decaying organic matter — rotting logs, compost, or dead palm trunks — playing an important role in nutrient cycling. The Coconut Rhinoceros Beetle (Oryctes rhinoceros) is a significant pest of coconut and oil palms across Asia.

Did you know? Rhinoceros beetles have been documented capable of lifting objects up to 850 times their own body weight — one of the highest strength-to-weight ratios recorded in the animal kingdom. For context, this would be equivalent to a human lifting approximately 65 tonnes.
Sandwood defoliator moth:
Scientific Name: Amata passalis (Fabricius, 1781)

Family: Erebidae (Subfamily: Arctiinae — Tiger & Lichen Moths)
Origin: Endemic to India and Sri Lanka
Most Commonly Found: Sandalwood forests and plantations in peninsular India, particularly Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh; also on alternate host plants
Wingspan: ~30–45 mm

Distinguishing Feature: A day-flying moth (diurnal) with bold, contrasting markings — dark forewings with white or yellow translucent spots, and a vivid yellow-and-black abdomen. The aposematic patterning warns predators of its chemical defences. Notable for its extreme multivoltinism: it completes 6–11 generations per year, with an average life cycle of just 62 days in captivity. A female lays approximately 305 eggs over her brief adult lifespan.
Habits & Habitat: Best known as the principal defoliator of Indian sandalwood (Santalum album), a tree of immense economic and cultural importance. Also recorded on alternate hosts including cowpeas, various pulses, cycas, and mulberry. Pupation occurs on the host plant. Adults emerge within one to two hours of sunrise and are ready to mate within a day. Active year-round with continuous broods.

Did you know? The Sandalwood Defoliator is one of the most significant threats to sandalwood plantations in India — capable of completely stripping trees of foliage. Its natural parasitoid, Apanteles nepitae, is being studied as a biological control agent to manage outbreaks without chemical pesticides.
Sphinx hawk moth:
Scientific Name: Family Sphingidae (Latreille, 1802)

Family: Sphingidae; ~1,450 species worldwide; 120+ species recorded in India
Origin: Worldwide, best represented in the tropics; highly diverse in tropical Asia and India
Most Commonly Found: Gardens, forest edges, and flowering habitats throughout India; adults active at dusk and dawn
Wingspan: 25–150 mm (species dependent)

Distinguishing Feature: Named for two behaviours: the larva's habit of rearing up in a "sphinx" posture when threatened, tucking its head beneath its body; and the adults' remarkably rapid, hummingbird-like hovering flight. Adults are heavy-bodied with long, pointed abdomens and narrow, elongated forewings. They possess an extraordinarily long proboscis — some species exceed 30 cm — enabling them to access nectar from deep, tubular flowers that no other pollinator can reach. Caterpillars bear a distinctive horn-like projection on the abdomen (hornworms).
Habits & Habitat: Most species are nocturnal or crepuscular, feeding on night-blooming flowers. Some diurnal species mimic hummingbirds or bumblebees in appearance and behaviour. Powerful fliers — among the fastest insects recorded. Found across diverse habitats from tropical rainforest to semi-arid scrub. Important pollinators; some orchids have evolved specifically for hawk moth pollination.

Did you know? Charles Darwin, on examining the Star of Bethlehem orchid (Angraecum sesquipedale) with its 30 cm nectar spur, predicted the existence of a hawk moth with a proboscis long enough to reach it. The moth — Xanthopan praedicta — was not confirmed until 130 years after his prediction.
Thyas:
Scientific Name: Thyas juno (Dalman, 1823) and related species

Family: Erebidae (Subfamily: Erebinae — Owlet Moths)
Origin: Tropical Asia; distributed across India, Sri Lanka, and Southeast Asia
Most Commonly Found: Tropical and subtropical forests and forest edges across India and Southeast Asia
Wingspan: ~60–80 mm

Distinguishing Feature: Cryptic, bark-like brown and grey forewings provide excellent camouflage when at rest on tree trunks. The hindwings, concealed during rest, are brilliantly coloured — typically vivid orange or yellow with contrasting dark markings — and are flash-exposed suddenly when the moth is disturbed, startling predators with an unexpected burst of colour. Belongs to the large Erebinae subfamily, which includes many piercing-mouthed fruit-feeding moths.
Habits & Habitat: Nocturnal and strongly attracted to light. Adults are documented feeders on fruit juices, using their proboscis to access rotting or ripening fruits. During the day, they rest motionless on tree bark, their forewings blending seamlessly with the substrate. Found in tropical and subtropical forest habitats. One of the few Erebinae genera successfully maintained in captivity by dedicated moth breeders due to their manageable biology.

Did you know? The sudden revelation of Thyas's brightly coloured hindwings — known as "flash colouration" — is a well-documented anti-predator strategy. The momentary visual shock dissorients predators, giving the moth a critical window to escape before the cryptic forewings conceal it again at rest.
7 spotted cockroach:
Scientific Name: Therea petiveriana (Linnaeus, 1758)

Family: Polyphagidae (a basal cockroach family); also called the Indian Domino Cockroach or Desert Cockroach
Origin: Endemic to peninsular India; type specimens originally collected from Madras (Chennai)
Most Commonly Found: Scrub forests and semi-arid zones of southern India; leaf litter in dry deciduous forests
Body Length: 2.5–3.5 cm

Distinguishing Feature — Mimicry: A remarkably beautiful cockroach: shiny jet-black body adorned with 6–7 bright white square-like spots — a striking pattern that mimics the aggressive ground beetle Anthia sexguttata, which possesses potent chemical spray defences. The spots on the asymmetrical wing covers (tegmina) are cleverly positioned so that, when closed, they appear perfectly symmetrical. The orange-yellow abdomen is hidden beneath the wings at rest.
Habits & Habitat: Crepuscular (active at dawn and dusk), spending the hottest hours burrowed under leaf litter or loose soil, sometimes to depths of 30 cm. A ground-dweller of scrub forests and dry deciduous woodlands. Detritivorous — scavenges on leaf litter, fallen fruit, and dead insects. Nymphs are subterranean, emerging progressively with development. Lifespan up to two years. Popular in the exotic pet trade due to its docile temperament and stunning appearance.

Did you know? The Seven-Spotted Cockroach harbours the ancient bacterium Blattabacterium in its gut — a symbiont that has co-evolved with cockroaches for over 200 million years and recycles nitrogen waste into essential amino acids, allowing the insect to thrive on nutrient-poor debris.
Tawny coster:
Scientific Name: Acraea terpsicore (Linnaeus, 1758)

Family: Nymphalidae (Tribe: Acraeini — one of only two Indian representatives of this predominantly African tribe)
Origin: South Asia, Southeast Asia; recently expanded its range to Australia — one of the fastest documented butterfly range expansions
Most Commonly Found: Open grassland, scrub, forest edges, and gardens wherever its host plant (Passiflora) grows across India and Sri Lanka
Wingspan: 53–64 mm

Distinguishing Feature: Rich tawny-orange wings with distinct black spots and a broad black terminal border containing a row of small white spots — worn with remarkable boldness due to chemical protection. The body is black with white polka dots. Flight is deliberately slow, weak, and unhurried — an advertisement of unpalatability rather than a vulnerability. The tough, leathery wing surface can withstand several pecks from a bird without tearing, allowing the butterfly to survive encounters and recover.
Habits & Habitat: Never found in dense shade — strongly prefers open, sunny habitats within 3 m of the ground. Wherever its larval host plant (Passiflora spp. — passion vines) is abundant, the Tawny Coster flourishes. Adults feed slowly and at length on flowers, often resting with wings open or half-closed. When attacked, the butterfly plays dead and secretes a noxious yellow fluid from leg glands containing cyanogenic compounds.

Did you know? The Tawny Coster expanded its range from South Asia into Australia at an average rate of ~135 km per year — and female-biased dispersal was documented at the leading edge of the expansion front, the first time this phenomenon had been recorded in any butterfly species.
